questions.question:
A six sided unbiased die with four green faces and two red faces is rolled seven times. Which of the following combinations is the most likely outcome of the experiment?

A. Three green faces and four red faces.
B. Four green faces and three red faces.
C. Five green faces and two red faces.
D. Six green faces and one red face.
questions.answer: C. Five green faces and two red faces.
questions.to_find: most likely combination of green and red faces in seven rolls

understand.plain: We have a biased die with P(green) = 4/6 = 2/3 and P(red) = 2/6 = 1/3. The die is rolled 7 times and we want to know which combination (k green, 7-k red) is most probable. This is a binomial distribution problem where we need to find the mode. The most likely outcome in a binomial(n, p) experiment is approximately n*p. Here n*p = 7 * (2/3) = 14/3 ≈ 4.67, so the mode is either 4 or 5.

keywords.explain: Models the number of successes in n independent trials each with probability p of success
keywords.example: Rolling a green face k times in 7 rolls follows Binomial(7, 2/3)

keywords.explain: The most likely value k, approximately equal to floor((n+1)*p) or related to n*p
keywords.example: For Binomial(7, 2/3), the mode is 5 because P(5 greens) is maximum

understand.visual_alt: A binomial distribution bar chart for 7 rolls of a die with P(green) = 2/3, showing peak at k = 5 green faces.

given.aim: identify the most probable combination of green and red face outcomes in 7 rolls

terms.meaning: probability of rolling a green face in one roll
terms.example: 4 green faces out of 6 total faces gives P(green) = 4/6 = 2/3
terms.connects: used as p in the binomial distribution

terms.meaning: probability of rolling a red face in one roll
terms.example: 2 red faces out of 6 total faces gives P(red) = 2/6 = 1/3
terms.connects: used as 1-p in the binomial distribution

terms.meaning: total number of independent trials
terms.example: the die is rolled exactly 7 times
terms.connects: determines the parameter n of Binomial(n, p)

terms.meaning: the value of k that maximizes the binomial probability P(X = k)
terms.example: compare P(3 green), P(4 green), P(5 green), P(6 green) to find which is largest
terms.connects: the mode of a Binomial(7, 2/3) distribution

given.plan: Compute P(k greens) = C(7,k) * (2/3)^k * (1/3)^(7-k) for k = 3, 4, 5, 6, and identify which is largest. Alternatively use the mode formula: mode = floor((n+1)*p) = floor(8 * 2/3) = floor(16/3) = 5.

steps.title: Set up the binomial model
steps.note: All probabilities share denominator 2187 = 3^7, so comparing numerators suffices.

steps.title: Compute numerators for each option
steps.note: k = 5 gives numerator 672, which is the largest among all options.

steps.title: Identify the most likely combination
steps.formula_raw: Most likely k = argmax P(X = k)
steps.apply: Comparing: 280 < 448 < 560 < 672. Maximum is at k = 5 greens, 2 reds. So P(5 green, 2 red) = 672/2187 is the highest.
steps.note: The mode of Binomial(n, p) can also be found as floor((n+1)*p) = floor(16/3) = 5, confirming k = 5.

solution.result: The most likely outcome is five green faces and two red faces (option C), with probability 672/2187 ≈ 0.307.

questions.formula_note: The binomial probability formula C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k) identifies k = 5 as the mode since its numerator C(7,5)*2^5 = 672 exceeds all other candidates.

questions.question:
There are five bags each containing identical sets of ten distinct chocolates. One chocolate is picked from each bag. The probability that at least two chocolates are identical is ________

A. 0.3024
B. 0.6235
C. 0.6976
D. 0.8225
questions.to_find: probability that at least two of the five picked chocolates are identical

understand.plain: Each bag has the same 10 distinct chocolates and one is picked from each of the 5 bags. Because the bags have identical sets, two picks are 'identical' when they are the same chocolate type. The event 'at least two identical' is the complement of 'all five are different types'. We compute P(all different) and subtract from 1.

keywords.explain: P(at least one match) = 1 - P(no match at all)
keywords.example: Easier to count the ways all 5 chocolates are different types than to count all cases with a duplicate

keywords.explain: Each bag yields one chocolate independently; total equally-likely outcomes = 10^5
keywords.example: The first bag has 10 choices, the second also 10, giving 10 * 10 * 10 * 10 * 10 = 100000 total outcomes

keywords.explain: Choosing one from 10 types in each of 5 trials is analogous to 5 birthdays among 10 days

understand.visual_alt: Five bags each containing 10 labeled chocolates, one drawn from each, illustrating the birthday-style probability setup.

given.aim: find P(at least two chocolates match in type among the five picks)

terms.meaning: each bag contains exactly 10 distinct types
terms.example: types labeled 1 through 10; both bags may yield type '3'
terms.connects: total equally-likely outcomes per pick = 10

terms.meaning: one chocolate picked from each of five bags independently
terms.example: total sample space size = 10^5 = 100000
terms.connects: denominator in probability calculation

terms.meaning: probability that all five chosen chocolates are of distinct types
terms.connects: complement event used to find P(at least two identical)

terms.meaning: P(at least two identical) = 1 - P(all different)
terms.connects: final answer

given.plan: Compute P(all five chocolates are of different types) = P(5,10)/10^5 = (10*9*8*7*6)/100000, then use the complement rule.

steps.title: Count favorable outcomes for all distinct
steps.note: Order matters here because the first pick is from bag 1, second from bag 2, etc.

steps.title: Total outcomes and P(all different)
steps.note: Each pick is independent so we multiply the 10 choices across 5 bags.

steps.title: Apply complement rule
steps.formula_raw: P(at least two identical) = 1 - P(all different)

solution.result: The probability that at least two chocolates are identical is 1 - 30240/100000 = 0.6976.

questions.formula_note: The complement approach avoids enumerating all overlapping cases; P(all distinct) = P(10,5)/10^5 = 0.3024 and subtracting from 1 gives 0.6976.

questions.question:
A box contains five balls of same size and shape. Three of them are green coloured balls and two of them are orange coloured balls. Balls are drawn from the box one at a time. If a green ball is drawn, it is not replaced. If an orange ball is drawn, it is replaced with another orange ball.

First ball is drawn. What is the probability of getting an orange ball in the next draw?

A. 1/3
B. 2/5
C. 23/50
D. 11/30
questions.to_find: probability of drawing an orange ball on the second draw

understand.plain: The box starts with 3 green and 2 orange balls (5 total). After the first draw, either a green ball was removed (leaving 2 green, 2 orange in 4 balls) or an orange ball was drawn and replaced by another orange (still 3 green, 2 orange in 5 balls — wait, actually re-reading: orange replaced with another orange means ball count stays 5). We use total probability by conditioning on the first draw result.

keywords.explain: P(B) = P(B|A)*P(A) + P(B|A^c)*P(A^c), partitioning on the first draw outcome
keywords.example: Condition on whether first ball was green or orange

keywords.explain: P(orange second | first was green) uses the updated composition after removing green
keywords.example: If first is green: box has 2G, 2O in 4 balls, so P(orange) = 2/4 = 1/2

keywords.explain: Orange replaced by orange keeps box composition same except green removal is permanent
keywords.example: If first is orange: box stays 3G, 2O in 5 balls, P(orange second) = 2/5

understand.visual_alt: A box with 3 green and 2 orange balls, showing two branches after the first draw: green removed versus orange replaced.

given.aim: find P(second ball is orange) using law of total probability over first draw outcome

terms.meaning: 3 green, 2 orange balls in box (5 total)
terms.example: P(first is green) = 3/5, P(first is orange) = 2/5
terms.connects: weights for the total probability formula

terms.meaning: green not replaced; box becomes 2G, 2O (4 balls)
terms.example: P(orange second | first green) = 2/4 = 1/2
terms.connects: contributes (3/5)*(1/2) = 3/10 to the total

terms.meaning: orange replaced by orange; box stays 3G, 2O (5 balls)
terms.example: P(orange second | first orange) = 2/5
terms.connects: contributes (2/5)*(2/5) = 4/25 to the total

terms.meaning: sum over all first-draw cases to get overall P(orange second)
terms.connects: leads to the final answer

given.plan: Partition on first draw (green or orange), compute P(orange second) in each case, combine using law of total probability.

steps.title: Second draw given first was green
steps.formula_raw: P(orange second | first green) = (orange in box) / (total in box)
steps.apply: Green removed, not replaced: box now has 2G + 2O = 4 balls. P(orange | first green) = 2/4 = 1/2.
steps.note: Box size shrinks to 4 when a green is removed.

steps.title: Second draw given first was orange
steps.formula_raw: P(orange second | first orange) = (orange in box) / (total in box)
steps.apply: Orange replaced by orange: box stays 3G + 2O = 5 balls. P(orange | first orange) = 2/5.
steps.note: Box size stays 5 when an orange is drawn and replaced.

questions.formula_note: Law of total probability splits on the first draw outcome: green (removes 1 green, box shrinks) vs orange (replaced, box unchanged). Combining gives P(orange second) = 23/50.

questions.question:
A fair six-faced dice, with the faces labelled 1', 2', 3', 4', 5', 6', and V, is rolled thrice. What is the probability of rolling V exactly once?

A. 25/72
B. 5/18
C. 25/216
D. 5/72
questions.to_find: probability of rolling face V exactly once in three rolls of a six-faced die

understand.plain: A six-faced die is labeled with six values, one of which is V. So P(V) = 1/6 and P(not V) = 5/6 on each roll. The die is rolled three times independently. We want the probability that V appears on exactly one of the three rolls. This is a binomial probability with n = 3 trials and p = 1/6.

keywords.explain: Each roll is a Bernoulli trial: success (V) with p = 1/6, failure with 1-p = 5/6
keywords.example: Three independent rolls give Binomial(3, 1/6) distribution

keywords.explain: The success (V) appears on exactly one of the three rolls; the other two show non-V faces
keywords.example: Ways this can happen: VXX, XVX, XXV — each equally likely, so multiply P(one specific arrangement) by C(3,1) = 3

keywords.explain: Rolls do not affect each other; probabilities multiply across rolls
keywords.example: P(V on roll 1, not-V on rolls 2 and 3) = (1/6)*(5/6)*(5/6) = 25/216

understand.visual_alt: A six-faced die with five numbered faces and one V-face, showing three successive rolls.

given.aim: compute P(V appears exactly once in 3 rolls) using binomial probability

terms.meaning: probability of rolling V on a single throw
terms.example: die has 6 faces, one labeled V
terms.connects: the success probability p in the binomial formula

terms.meaning: probability of NOT rolling V on a single throw
terms.example: five faces are non-V
terms.connects: the failure probability 1-p in the binomial formula

terms.meaning: the die is rolled three times independently
terms.example: each roll is independent with the same probabilities
terms.connects: the number of trials in Binomial(3, 1/6)

terms.meaning: V appears exactly once
terms.example: could be first, second, or third roll
terms.connects: the target count in the binomial probability formula

given.plan: Use binomial probability formula P(X = 1) = C(3,1) * (1/6)^1 * (5/6)^2.

steps.title: Identify binomial parameters
steps.note: The six faces include exactly one V face, so p = 1/6.

steps.title: Compute the binomial probability
steps.note: Simplify 75/216 by dividing both by 3 to get 25/72.

questions.formula_note: Binomial probability P(X=1) = C(3,1)*(1/6)*(5/6)^2 = 25/72 directly gives the answer; no complement or conditioning needed.

questions.question:
Two identical cube shaped dice each with faces numbered 1 to 6 are rolled simultaneously. The probability that an even number is rolled out on each dice is:

A. 1/36
B. 1/12
C. 1/2
D. 1/4
questions.to_find: probability that both dice show an even number when rolled simultaneously

understand.plain: Each standard die has faces 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. The even faces are 2, 4, 6 — exactly 3 out of 6. Since the two dice are rolled independently, we multiply probabilities. P(even on one die) = 3/6 = 1/2. P(even on both dice) = (1/2) * (1/2) = 1/4.

keywords.explain: Two dice rolls do not influence each other; P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B)
keywords.example: P(die 1 even AND die 2 even) = P(die 1 even) * P(die 2 even)

keywords.explain: Faces 2, 4, 6 are even — 3 out of 6 faces
For independent events, the joint probability is the product of individual probabilities

understand.visual_alt: Two standard six-faced dice shown side by side, with even-numbered faces (2, 4, 6) highlighted.

given.aim: find the probability that both dice simultaneously show an even number

terms.meaning: each of the 6 faces (1 through 6) is equally likely
terms.example: P(any specific face) = 1/6
terms.connects: basis for computing P(even)

terms.meaning: faces 2, 4, 6 — three out of six faces are even
terms.example: P(even on one die) = 3/6 = 1/2
terms.connects: the marginal probability for each die

terms.meaning: the outcome of one die does not affect the other
terms.example: total sample space has 6*6 = 36 equally-likely outcomes
terms.connects: allows use of multiplication rule for joint probability

terms.meaning: probability both dice show even simultaneously

given.plan: Compute P(die 1 even) * P(die 2 even) using independence, or count favorable outcomes (9) out of 36 total.

steps.title: Probability of even on a single die
steps.apply: Even faces: {2, 4, 6} — count = 3. Total faces = 6. P(even) = 3/6 = 1/2.
steps.note: Each face is equally likely for a fair die.

steps.title: Apply multiplication rule for independent events
steps.formula_raw: P(both even) = P(die 1 even) * P(die 2 even)
steps.note: The two dice rolls are independent so we multiply. Equivalently, 9 favorable outcomes out of 36 total gives 9/36 = 1/4.

questions.formula_note: Independence of the two dice allows direct multiplication: P(1/2) * P(1/2) = 1/4; equivalently 9 even-even pairs out of 36 total outcomes.

questions.question:
The probability of a boy or a girl being born is 1/2. For a family having only three children, what is the probability of having two girls and one boy?

A. 3/8
B. 1/8
C. 1/4
D. 1/2
questions.to_find: probability that a three-child family has exactly two girls and one boy

understand.plain: Each child is independently born as a boy or girl with equal probability 1/2. Having 'two girls and one boy' means exactly 2 of the 3 births result in girls. This is a binomial distribution problem with n = 3 trials, p = 1/2, and we want k = 2 successes (girls). The answer is C(3, 2) * (1/2)^2 * (1/2)^1 = 3 * 1/8 = 3/8.

keywords.explain: P(X = k) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k) for n independent Bernoulli trials
Each child independently is a boy or girl with p = 1/2
C(3,2) = 3 counts the number of positions where the 2 girls can appear among 3 births
keywords.example: GGB, GBG, BGG — three arrangements have exactly 2 girls

understand.visual_alt: A probability tree with three levels for three births, highlighting all branches leading to exactly two girls and one boy.

given.aim: compute P(exactly 2 girls out of 3 children) using binomial probability

terms.meaning: probability of a girl on each birth
terms.example: P(boy) = P(girl) = 1/2
terms.connects: the success probability p in the binomial model

terms.meaning: total number of independent births
terms.example: sample space has 2^3 = 8 equally-likely birth sequences
terms.connects: number of trials in the binomial distribution

terms.meaning: exactly two births result in girls
terms.example: arrangements: GGB, GBG, BGG — 3 sequences
terms.connects: the target count k = 2 in the binomial formula

terms.meaning: number of ways to choose which 2 of the 3 children are girls
terms.connects: the binomial coefficient that accounts for different birth orders

given.plan: Use binomial probability P(X = 2) = C(3,2) * (1/2)^2 * (1/2)^1 with n = 3, p = 1/2.

steps.title: Identify the favorable sequences
steps.formula_raw: C(n, k) = number of arrangements of k successes in n trials
steps.apply: n = 3, k = 2 girls. C(3, 2) = 3. Sequences: GGB, GBG, BGG — three arrangements.
steps.note: Each arrangement has exactly 2 girls and 1 boy.

steps.title: Compute probability of each arrangement
steps.apply: P(GGB) = (1/2)*(1/2)*(1/2) = 1/8. Same for GBG and BGG since p = 1-p = 1/2.
steps.note: All 8 sequences are equally likely when p = 1/2.

steps.title: Apply binomial probability formula
steps.note: Since p = 1/2, this simplifies to C(3,2)/2^3 = 3/8.

solution.result: P(exactly 2 girls and 1 boy in 3 children) = 3/8.

questions.formula_note: Binomial formula C(3,2)*(1/2)^3 = 3/8 counts 3 favorable sequences (GGB, GBG, BGG) out of 8 equally-likely birth sequences.
